title: Why Ridge Keeps All Features
concept: Ridge regression keeps all features in the model by shrinking their weights near zero, rather than eliminating features completely.

exercises.prompt: Recap: what is the main risk of setting the regularization parameter (alpha) too high?

options.text: The model will underfit and become too simple to learn the real patterns
The model will overfit and become highly unstable
The model will run out of memory

exercises.explain: Excellent! Setting alpha too high forces weights too close to zero, leading to underfitting. Now, let's look at how Ridge treats our features as it shrinks their weights.

cards.title: The quiet choir
cards.body: Imagine a choir where some singers are loud and others are quiet. If you ask everyone to sing softer, everyone lowers their volume, but no one stops singing. They all still contribute to the overall sound.

cards.title: Ridge Keeps Every Feature
cards.body: Because Ridge (L2) regression uses a squared penalty, it shrinks weights close to zero but never sets them to exactly zero. This means every single input feature remains in the model.

cards.title: No weeds pulled
cards.body: Instead of pulling out weak plants (dropping features), Ridge just trims them all down. Every plant stays in the garden, even the tiny ones, contributing a little bit to the final view.

cards.title: Thousands of small signals
cards.body: If you predict health risks using genes, thousands of genes might play a tiny, subtle role. Dropping genes would lose information. Ridge keeps them all, giving each a small weight.

cards.title: Is dropping features always better?
cards.body: We might think we should always delete useless-looking features. But if a feature has even a tiny bit of real signal, keeping it with a tiny weight (as Ridge does) often yields better predictions.

cards.title: Feature Selection vs. Ridge
cards.body: How Ridge compares to dropping features entirely:

right.text:
Keeps all 100 features.
Shrinks weights of weaker ones.

exercises.prompt: What happens to the weights of less important features in Ridge regression?

options.text: They shrink close to zero, but remain in the model
They are set to exactly zero and dropped
They grow larger to compensate for other features
They are replaced with random numbers

exercises.explain: Ridge shrinks weights of weaker features close to zero, but it never sets them to exactly zero, so they are never dropped.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Ridge regression is a good choice when you believe almost every feature in your dataset has at least some small predictive value.
exercises.explain: True! Since Ridge keeps all features, it is perfect when you want to retain many small signals rather than selecting a few.

exercises.sentence: Unlike methods that select features by dropping them, Ridge regression shrinks weights but keeps ___ features.
exercises.explain: Ridge keeps all features, ensuring that no potential signal is completely discarded from the model.

exercises.prompt: Which weight set is a result of Ridge regression, where no feature is completely removed?
exercises.explain: The first set contains only non-zero weights, meaning all features are kept. The second set has zeros, which is not typical for Ridge.

exercises.prompt: You are predicting customer satisfaction using 50 features. You suspect that many of these features are highly correlated and individually weak, but collectively they contain the full story. Which method makes the most sense?

options.text: Ridge regression, because it retains all 50 features while shrinking their weights to prevent overfitting
Standard linear regression, to let the weights swing as large as they need to
Dropping 45 features and running regression on the remaining 5
Removing all features and predicting the average score

exercises.explain: Exactly! Since you want to retain the collective story of all 50 weak/correlated features without letting weights blow up, Ridge is the perfect tool. 🎉
